subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Master’s Degree, completed at age 22
Degree focused on finance, business, or a related quantitative field
Undergraduate studies included coursework in economics and data analysis
Participated in extracurriculars such as university investment clubs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Current title: Investment Fund Manager at OpenCore Ventures
Role centers on sourcing and evaluating open source projects for investment
Acts as an advisor to portfolio company founders
Responsible for operational oversight of portfolio companies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works at the intersection of venture capital and open source technology
Primary industry: Venture-backed technology investment
Focus on early-stage startups built around open source projects

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: OpenCore Ventures is a small-to-mid-size firm with under 50 employees
Operates in a highly collaborative, startup-like environment
Lean teams with interns supporting research-heavy workflows

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Invests in and incubates companies based on open source software projects
Market segment: Early-stage tech startups and software infrastructure
Business model involves both funding and operational support

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 3-5 years of professional experience in investment management
Joined OpenCore Ventures shortly after completing Master’s degree
Current role held for approximately 2-3 years

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Works at an early-career to mid-level manager position
Directly manages interns and supports founders in an advisory capacity
Acts as a primary point of contact for open source project outreach

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Researches emerging and existing open source projects for investment potential
Conducts outreach to open source contributors and project authors
Advises founders on company formation and go-to-market strategy
Oversees operational processes for portfolio companies (e.g., scaling, hiring)

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends roughly 50% of time on research and due diligence
Around 20% devoted to outreach and networking with open source communities
Approximately 20% advising founders and supporting portfolio operations
Remainder on administrative tasks and fund operations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Has significant autonomy in identifying and researching investment opportunities
Final investment decisions made collaboratively with senior partners
Independently manages research interns and their workflows

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Quickly identifies promising projects on platforms like GitHub and Hacker News
Comfortable using LLMs to generate boilerplate research documents
Efficiently coordinates team communications via Slack

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Assessing the commercial viability of highly technical open source projects
Building trust and rapport with diverse open source contributors
Helping founders navigate scaling challenges and community management

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Collecting contributor names and engagement analytics from project repositories
Generating preliminary research templates using OpenAI's API
Data enrichment tasks using tools like Clay

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys deep dives into new open source projects and emerging communities
Finds strategic advising and founder mentorship highly engaging
Considers repetitive data gathering and template filling tedious

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: AI-generated research sometimes contains hallucinations, requiring manual review
Integrating data across multiple tools (e.g., HubSpot, Google Drive) can be clunky
Manual data entry for project analytics is time-consuming

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Productivity is highest when working on research and strategic advising, which are also the most enjoyable
Skill level in using automation tools increases enjoyment by reducing busywork
Tedious or repetitive tasks are less enjoyable, even when performed efficiently

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Highly enthusiastic about adopting new tech that increases efficiency
Actively seeks out and experiments with emerging tools, especially in AI
Encourages team members to share new tool discoveries

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to new software platforms and workflow changes
Able to integrate multiple new tools into daily work with minimal friction
Regularly updates processes as new technologies become available

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Invests time in self-directed learning and online tutorials for new tools
Prefers hands-on experimentation over lengthy formal training
Participates in webinars or workshops when relevant to immediate job needs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by direct productivity gains and process improvements
Occasional barrier: high workload can limit time for deep learning of complex tools
Less likely to explore tools without clear relevance to open source or investment tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers and leaders at OpenCore Ventures encourage experimentation with new technologies
Team culture values sharing and collective vetting of new tools
Management supports tool adoption if it streamlines core workflows

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Slack for real-time team and project communications
HubSpot for managing research pipelines and contributor outreach
Google Drive for document collaboration and storage
Carta for cap table management
Asana for project management and task tracking

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Greenhouse for recruiting and applicant tracking
Clay for data enrichment of leads and contacts
Loom for recording video demos and walkthroughs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Proficient with venture capital and startup operations tools (Carta, HubSpot)
Regularly uses GitHub to investigate open source project activity
Familiar with integration between project management and communication platforms

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasional friction when syncing data between HubSpot and Google Drive
Manual effort required to consolidate analytics across platforms
Some limitations in automating workflows between Asana and Slack

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands basic concepts such as large language models, training data, and prompt engineering
Can distinguish between supervised and unsupervised learning at a high level
Keeps up with major AI trends relevant to investment and open source

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Daily use of OpenAI’s API for auto-generating research document drafts
Occasionally leverages AI-powered features in HubSpot (e.g., email categorization)
Uses Clay’s AI-driven enrichment for contact and contributor data

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses LLMs every day to produce boilerplate research on new projects
Applies AI to automate repetitive data gathering and enrichment tasks
Relies on AI outputs as starting points, then reviews for accuracy

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI-generated research templates increase speed by 30-40% over manual drafting
Has encountered hallucinated facts in LLM outputs, requiring careful human review
Successful AI use has allowed scaling research capacity to more projects simultaneously

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: No formal in-house AI training program; relies on peer knowledge sharing
Team members frequently share prompts and best practices for LLM use
Occasionally seeks guidance from more technical colleagues on optimizing AI workflows

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers independent use of AI for initial drafts, followed by manual refinement
Uses AI as a first-pass assistant rather than a real-time collaborator
Occasionally works iteratively with AI when prompts require tuning

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Treats AI primarily as an assistant to automate repetitive or data-heavy steps
Relies on human judgment for final decisions and nuanced analysis
Views AI as a tool for scaling capacity, not as a substitute for expertise

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Very open to integrating new AI tools and automations into daily work
Advocates for experimentation with emerging AI features
Comfortable adjusting workflows to incorporate AI-driven efficiencies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for brainstorming and content generation
Uses command-based prompts for structured, repeatable tasks
Finds hybrid interfaces (e.g., guided chat with advanced settings) most flexible

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Assessing project viability often requires nuanced understanding of open source communities
Some tasks, like founder advising, depend on soft skills and industry experience
Routine data collection is less reliant on domain expertise and more suitable for automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for generating initial research drafts, but always verifies outputs
Does not rely solely on AI for investment or strategic decisions
Recognizes that AI can miss context or misinterpret technical signals

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Favors AI automation for repetitive, structured information gathering
Prefers human analysis for complex or high-stakes decisions
Sees value in blended workflows: AI for speed, humans for quality control

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: High automation is acceptable for low-impact, low-risk data tasks
Would be concerned if AI were used to make final investment decisions without oversight
Prefers to retain human oversight on tasks involving judgment or interpersonal interaction

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: AI enhances research and data analysis skills by increasing throughput
Soft skills such as networking and founder advising remain critical and irreplaceable
Routine administrative skills have become less central due to automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Prompt engineering and effective query formulation have become valuable
Ability to critically review and edit AI outputs is now essential
Increased need to understand data sources and model limitations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Minimal concern about AI replacing the core investment manager role
Recognizes that some research tasks may become fully automated over time
Sees AI as a tool for amplification, not replacement

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Manual review and editing are built into all AI-augmented workflows
Organizational process requires human sign-off on investment decisions
Team culture encourages skepticism and double-checking of AI-generated facts

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: AI adoption is increasing, with a bias toward responsible implementation
Company encourages experimentation but expects accountability for outputs
No blanket automation: human oversight remains a policy requirement

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will fundamentally reshape research and due diligence in venture capital
Anticipates greater access to information and more efficient market analysis
Sees potential for AI to democratize startup funding and reduce barriers for founders

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Concerned about AI hallucinations and the risk of propagating false information
Supports transparent use of AI, with clear disclosure to stakeholders
Believes human judgment should remain central in ethical decision-making

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Would benefit from AI that can reliably assess open source community health
Desires better multi-tool integration and automated workflow chaining
Wants more explainable AI outputs for due diligence transparency

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects research tasks to become almost fully automated, freeing time for strategic work
Foresees a shift toward more high-touch founder advising and relationship building
Anticipates the need to develop new skills in AI oversight and prompt design

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Implement structured review processes for AI-generated outputs
Invest in internal AI literacy and upskilling programs
Prioritize development of custom AI tools tailored to open source investment workflows
Establish guidelines for ethical and transparent AI deployment
